title: Making a simple timber balustrade infill panel
subtitle: Cut and fix a framed timber panel to fill an open balustrade gap safely
excerpt: How to measure, cut, and fit a solid timber infill panel between the handrail and stair string or floor at a staircase balustrade, meeting the building regulation requirement that no opening exceeds 100 mm. Covers the frame, panel, fixing, and finishing.
sourceNotes: Building Regulations Approved Document K (UK): protection from falling. City and Guilds Level 2 Site Carpentry. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: Material fitted between the handrail and the stair string, floor, or lower rail to close open gaps in a balustrade. UK Building Regulations Approved Document K require that no opening in a balustrade alongside a stair exceeds 100 mm in any direction, so that a 100 mm sphere cannot pass through. A solid panel, closely spaced spindles, or sheet material all satisfy this requirement.

content.text: is the simplest way to close an open balustrade. It needs no spindle layout, no turning, and the panel is a single cut piece. The frame that holds it is where care is needed. Stair openings are rarely square because the string runs at the stair pitch angle, so the panel is usually a parallelogram. Measure both dimensions twice before cutting.

18 mm MDF or plywood for the infill panel. 44 × 19 mm PAR (planed-all-round) softwood for the frame. PVA wood glue. Screws (40 mm No. 8 for frame jointing; 60 mm No. 10 for fixing to the handrail and string). Decorator's filler and paint to finish.

Measure the height of the opening at three points: at the newel post end, at mid-span, and at the other newel or wall end. Measure the width along the underside of the handrail. The bottom edge of the panel will follow the pitch of the stair string; measure the angle with the combination square set against the string and transfer this angle to the panel. Mark the panel shape on the MDF or plywood, check all dimensions again, and cut.

Cut the four frame members from 44 × 19 mm PAR softwood: a top rail, a bottom rail, and two uprights. The top rail fits against the underside of the handrail; the bottom rail fits against the top of the stair string or the floor. Mitre or butt-joint the corners. Drill pilot holes and countersink screw positions. Glue and screw the frame together, checking it is square at the corners with the combination square.
Step 3, fit the panel into the frame

Apply PVA glue to the inner face of the frame rebate or to the edge of the panel, position the panel, and clamp or pin it in place. Wipe away any excess glue. Allow the glue to cure before handling. Check the panel sits flush on both faces of the frame; if it protrudes on one side, mark and sand the proud edge.
Step 4, fix into the balustrade opening

Offer the framed panel up to the opening and check the fit. Fix the top rail to the underside of the handrail with screws driven up through the rail into the handrail timber. Fix the bottom rail to the string or floor. Check the panel is plumb in both directions. Fill all screw heads with decorator's filler, sand flush when cured, and paint to match the surrounding balustrade.

attrs.symptom: Panel rattles or flexes when pushed
attrs.cause: The frame is not screwed tightly enough to the handrail or string, or the panel is not bonded fully into the frame.
attrs.fix: Check that all screws are driven fully home into solid timber. Re-glue any panel edge that has lifted from the frame and clamp until cured.

attrs.symptom: The panel shape is slightly wrong and leaves a gap at the string
attrs.cause: The stair pitch angle was transferred incorrectly or the measurement was taken at the wrong point.
attrs.fix: If the gap is less than 5 mm, fill and paint. If it is larger, cut a fillet from matching timber to the correct angle, glue it to the bottom edge of the panel, sand flush, and paint.
